content:
I am working on writing a new Platform Driver to replace GBridge (related to Greybus) as a part of GSoC 2023. I have previously worked on implementing Rust Std for UEFI as a part of GSoC 2022. Thus I am quite adept at Rust core and alloc along with unsafe and FFI.

While I would like to use Rust, I do have some questions/reservations if it will be the right fit for my project. Some of these questions might just be due to lack of Kernel development experience:

  1. Can a platform driver like this be a loadable kernel module? (I think it can based on the documentation).
  2. Rust for linux seems to require building the kernel with LLVM. However, most distros use gcc (I think) to build the kernel. So can a Rust module still work with such a kernel? Does that need any extra config options to be enabled?
I'm trying to find some more information on GBridge, but searching keeps giving me garbage. Do you have a link for what this is?

I think my GSOC Proposal should help with that.
I also found this article that might be useful.

I believe building the rest of the kernel with GCC is possible, but it tends to break as new options are passed to GCC that clang doesn't understand. We use rust-bindgen for generating bindings which is based on libclang and as such inherits all clang C compiler flags. I believe we have a list of options only GCC supports to skip when invoking rust-bindgen, but this list is not always up to date.
Rustc itself uses LLVM unconditionally currently. There is an experimental GCC backend though.

Regarding the first question, my answer would be "partially". For the Android Binder driver, it causes various issues, so we don't use it and have to disable the sample, which is normally a module (this is related to MODVERSIONS afair)
or maybe it had to do with CFI?
So would it be possible for me to build a Rust module and load it into my current normal Kernel? (As long as I generate bindings against the current kenrel)
I know that Asahi Linux has drivers written in Rust but I'm not sure how that works. Does it build the kernel using LLVM?
In the case of Android, we are already building the kernel with LLVM, so for us gcc is not relevant.

Alice Ryhl said:

In the case of Android, we are already building the kernel with LLVM, so for us gcc is not relevant.

Cool. but I don't think the Greybus host will ever be Android. WIll probably be a normal linux or an embedded linux system.
Part of the rust infrastructure (like libcore and libkernel) is linked into the main kernel image. As such you need to build the kernel itself with rust support to be able to load rust modules.
I see. Are there any distros that actually enable Rust support? I do remember hearing Manjaro got some support for M1 macs. Were they only using the C drivers from Asahi and not the Rust ones?
Ubuntu 23.04 does: https://www.phoronix.com/news/Ubuntu-23.04-Rust-Linux-Kernel
That's definitely great. If Ubuntu is doing it, all Ubuntu based distros will probably get it as well

Ayush Singh said:

I know that Asahi Linux has drivers written in Rust but I'm not sure how that works. Does it build the kernel using LLVM?

The Arch builds are using GCC for the C part of the kernel, and yes the GPU driver is a loadable module. However, you can't have Rust support itself as a loadable module right now (with the upstream trees anyway). So the kernel itself and all the core abstractions are always built-in, then your driver can be a module like any other driver.

Right now upstream doesn't have enough abstractions for platform support though, so you will have to use downstream trees. Hopefully this will change in the next few kernel versions ^^

I think it should be fine as long as I can get stuff merged to the linux-next (rust-next) branch by the end. Besides, there doesn't seem to be any strong objections from BeagleBoard community members, so I guess it might be fine. Still, feel free to inform me if there are any more limitations than LLVM and limited architectures and probably having to generate bindings

Asahi Lina said:

Ayush Singh said:

I know that Asahi Linux has drivers written in Rust but I'm not sure how that works. Does it build the kernel using LLVM?

The Arch builds are using GCC for the C part of the kernel, and yes the GPU driver is a loadable module. However, you can't have Rust support itself as a loadable module right now (with the upstream trees anyway). So the kernel itself and all the core abstractions are always built-in, then your driver can be a module like any other driver.

Right now upstream doesn't have enough abstractions for platform support though, so you will have to use downstream trees. Hopefully this will change in the next few kernel versions ^^

I talked to John Meneghini from Red Hat. He said that if Rust kernel modules are ever coming to RHEL, it would have to be as a loadable modules. If they will ever support rust they will want the option of shipping kernels entirely without Rust with all Rust stuff in the loadable modules. So maybe it is not a bad idea to consider how to achieve this @Miguel Ojeda

bjorn3 said:

Ubuntu 23.04 does: https://www.phoronix.com/news/Ubuntu-23.04-Rust-Linux-Kernel

Interesting. I guess they are building with LLVM then? I do not think it is supported to build the kernel image with GCC and then modules with LLVM. Anyway, there is Rust in the kernel image, so they would have to build those parts with LLVM and then link the GCC/LLVM bits together in the end. As far as I know that is not supported by kconfig/kbuild.

Rustc has an entirely different commandline interface from clang anyway, so it doesn't matter that kbuild passes different args to the C compiler when using GCC vs LLVM. (except for in rust-bindgen, but there we can and do filter out unsupported args on a best effort basis) Linking itself shouldn't matter unless LTO is used.

Andreas Hindborg said:

Asahi Lina said:

Ayush Singh said:

I know that Asahi Linux has drivers written in Rust but I'm not sure how that works. Does it build the kernel using LLVM?

The Arch builds are using GCC for the C part of the kernel, and yes the GPU driver is a loadable module. However, you can't have Rust support itself as a loadable module right now (with the upstream trees anyway). So the kernel itself and all the core abstractions are always built-in, then your driver can be a module like any other driver.

Right now upstream doesn't have enough abstractions for platform support though, so you will have to use downstream trees. Hopefully this will change in the next few kernel versions ^^

I talked to John Meneghini from Red Hat. He said that if Rust kernel modules are ever coming to RHEL, it would have to be as a loadable modules. If they will ever support rust they will want the option of shipping kernels entirely without Rust with all Rust stuff in the loadable modules. So maybe it is not a bad idea to consider how to achieve this Miguel Ojeda

I don't think the Red Hat folks get to decide how the kernel modularity works... there's plenty of things in the kernel that are only supported built-in, just like Rust today. If they're concerned about that, it sounds like they should send an email to the mailing list to have that discussion ^^

The plan is to eventually make Rust abstractions for modular kernel components modular themselves, but I'm not sure anyone is considering making all of CONFIG_RUST modular right now...

Asahi Lina said:

Andreas Hindborg said:

Asahi Lina said:

Ayush Singh said:

I know that Asahi Linux has drivers written in Rust but I'm not sure how that works. Does it build the kernel using LLVM?

The Arch builds are using GCC for the C part of the kernel, and yes the GPU driver is a loadable module. However, you can't have Rust support itself as a loadable module right now (with the upstream trees anyway). So the kernel itself and all the core abstractions are always built-in, then your driver can be a module like any other driver.

Right now upstream doesn't have enough abstractions for platform support though, so you will have to use downstream trees. Hopefully this will change in the next few kernel versions ^^

I talked to John Meneghini from Red Hat. He said that if Rust kernel modules are ever coming to RHEL, it would have to be as a loadable modules. If they will ever support rust they will want the option of shipping kernels entirely without Rust with all Rust stuff in the loadable modules. So maybe it is not a bad idea to consider how to achieve this Miguel Ojeda

I don't think the Red Hat folks get to decide how the kernel modularity works... there's plenty of things in the kernel that are only supported built-in, just like Rust today. If they're concerned about that, it sounds like they should send an email to the mailing list to have that discussion ^^

The plan is to eventually make Rust abstractions for modular kernel components modular themselves, but I'm not sure anyone is considering making all of CONFIG_RUST modular right now...

They might not get to decide what upstream does, but they certainly can influence and guide. What RHEL does is an entirely different thing, since RHEL is practically a fork of Linux. They can do whatever they want.

My point is that if we can make decisions that make it easier for us to enter RHEL or other products, it might be worth giving those decisions some attention. I'm not saying that we should blindly follow requests made by vendors. But let's discuss at least.

Ayush Singh said:

The Arch builds are using GCC for the C part of the kernel, and yes the GPU driver is a loadable module. What is the reason for this (rather strange) requirement? Is it because of licensing or something weird like that?

So the kernel itself and all the core abstractions are always built-in, then your driver can be a module like any other driver.

Right now upstream doesn't have enough abstractions for platform support though, so you will have to use downstream trees. Hopefully this will change in the next few kernel versions ^^

I talked to John Meneghini from Red Hat. He said that if Rust kernel modules are ever coming to RHEL, it would have to be as a loadable modules. If they will ever support rust they will want the option of shipping kernels entirely without Rust with all Rust stuff in the loadable modules. So maybe it is not a bad idea to consider how to achieve this Miguel Ojeda

What is the reason for this (rather strange) requirement? Is it because of licensing or something weird like that?

The conversation was a while back, but as far as I recall John said that all their support engineers would have to know Rust for them to ship Rust. I think debugging and fixing issues for customers is part of their business model. But this is just what John said, I do not know if it is official Red Hat policy. You should reach out to John if you want more details I think.
